part_a___general_details.name: Thomas J. Cartwright
part_a___general_details.product_name: AgriTrace UltraFlex HDX Cattle Primary Tag
part_a___general_details.ear_tag_position_cattle: Left ear, placed in the middle third of the ear between the upper and lower cartilage ridges, approximately 1.5 cm from the head, following the manufacturer’s diagram. Majority of affected animals have tags on the left ear; a smaller number on the right show similar issues.
part_a___general_details.details_of_findings:
I am reporting concerns regarding both welfare and performance with a batch of AgriTrace UltraFlex HDX Cattle Primary Tags supplied in February 2025.

Herd and context:
- Dairy herd of 165 Holstein-Friesian cattle on a family-run farm.
- Approximately 58 calves were tagged with this specific batch between 2025-03-02 and 2025-03-18.
- Tags were applied by myself (experienced, tagging cattle for over 15 years) and my son, who has also been trained in proper tagging technique. We followed the manufacturer’s instructions and used the matching AgriTrace applicator supplied with the order.

Timing of attachment:
- Calves were tagged within 24–72 hours of birth as per usual farm practice.
- All tags were disinfected in an iodine-based solution before application.
- Ears were cleaned with a clean disposable paper towel to remove visible dirt or moisture before tagging.

Welfare issues observed:
- Within 7–10 days of tagging, we started to see swelling and redness around the tag site on a number of calves.
- By 2025-03-25, 14 out of the 58 tagged calves showed varying degrees of irritation:
  - 6 calves with moderate swelling and warm tissue around the puncture site.
  - 5 calves with crusting and dried discharge around the tag stem.
  - 3 calves with more serious lesions: thick yellow discharge, hair loss around the site, and the animals were clearly sensitive when the ear was handled.
- In 2 of those serious cases, the tag had partially torn the ear tissue, leaving a small split at the lower edge of the hole.
- We involved our local vet (Nantwich Farm Vets) on 2025-03-27. The vet cleaned and treated the worst-affected ears with a topical antibiotic spray and advised close monitoring.
- No systemic illness (fever, loss of appetite) was noted, but the affected calves were noticeably head-shy when the ear was touched, suggesting discomfort.

Performance issues (losses and breakages):
- As of 2025-05-15, we have recorded the following losses/breakages from the original 58 tags:
  - 9 tags have been completely lost (the ear hole remains, but the tag is missing).
  - 4 tags have broken at the narrow neck between the panel and the stem; the round male part is still in the ear, but the main visual panel is gone.
- Losses started to be noticed around 3–5 weeks after application. We do not have excessive rubbing points in the calf pens (standard metal gates with rounded edges, no sharp projections identified).
- We have previously used a different brand of tags in the same housing and management system and did not experience this level of loss or breakage. Historically, our annual loss rate has been around 2–3%; with this batch we are already above 20% in under three months.
- The plastic on the broken tags appears more brittle than on our previous tags. Two broken tags snapped when we gently tried to twist them to read the numbers, which is not something we have encountered before.

Readability and printing quality:
- The laser printing on several tags has started to fade or blur sooner than expected.
- On 7 tags, the black printed characters have become noticeably lighter and less crisp within two months of application, despite the animals being housed indoors for most of that period.
- One specific example: calf UK123456 0015’s tag number is now difficult to read from a standard working distance (about 1.5–2 metres). We have to bring the calf into the race and stand close to confirm the number, which is not practical and increases handling stress.
- We clean tags occasionally with a damp cloth when reading in the parlour; this has never caused problems with other brands.

Application details and technique:
- We used only the official AgriTrace UltraFlex applicator recommended for this product.
- Applicator was checked before use to ensure correct alignment of male and female parts.
- Tags were applied through the central pre-marked area, avoiding the major veins and cartilage ridges as per the diagram.
- Ears were not excessively thick; these are young calves at 1–3 days old, a stage we have been tagging at for years without previous complication.
- We did not notice any misfires or obvious incorrect placements at the time of tagging.

Numbers affected (summary):
- Total animals tagged with this batch: 58 calves.
- Welfare issues (swelling/lesions): 14 animals (approx. 24%).
- Serious lesions requiring veterinary attention: 3 animals (approx. 5%).
- Tag losses (complete): 9 tags.
- Tag breakages (panel separated from stem): 4 tags.
- Readability issues (fading/blurred print): at least 7 tags to date.

Other relevant information:
- Batch/lot number printed on the packaging: AT-ULTRAFLEX-HDX-2025-02-B3 (best as I can read it from the label; I still have one unopened bag from the same batch stored in the office).
- Storage conditions: tags stored indoors in a dry office, out of direct sunlight, at normal room temperature (approx. 10–20°C) before use.
- We have not exposed tags or applicator to harsh chemicals, only standard disinfectant dips recommended for livestock equipment.
- We have been using AgriTrace products for about three years. Earlier batches performed acceptably, with only occasional losses. This is the first time we have seen this combination of infection, brittleness and poor print durability.

Impact on farm operations:
- We have had to re-tag several calves, which is additional handling and stress for the animals and extra time for us.
- The infections, although mostly localised, have required veterinary advice and treatment, adding to our costs.
- Inconsistent readability is making routine identification in the calf pens and during weighing more difficult and slower.

What we are hoping for:
- We would like this report to be shared with AgriTrace Identification Systems Ltd so they can investigate whether there is a manufacturing issue with this particular batch (AT-ULTRAFLEX-HDX-2025-02-B3).
- We would appreciate advice on whether the remaining unused tags from this batch should be used or returned, and whether any replacement or credit can be offered for the tags that have failed.
- If there are updated application recommendations specific to this product (e.g., slightly different placement or ear thickness guidelines), we would be grateful to receive them.

I am happy to provide photographs of the affected ears, the broken tags, and the packaging if this would assist the investigation, and I am willing to be contacted by phone or email for any further details.
